coredata.dc:description: Achilles tendinopathy (AT), which is characterized by aseptic inflammation, is one of the most common types of tendon injury. Chronic inflammation can worsen AT progression and interfere with tendon repair, and most current anti-inflammatory treatments are associated with adverse effects. Therefore, improving the inflammatory microenvironment and promoting tenogenesis remain major challenges in AT treatment. In this study, melatonin carbon dots (MCDs) combined with an injectable porcine small intestinal submucosal hydrogel (SIS gel) were developed for the minimally invasive treatment of AT. The anti-inflammatory effects and tenogenic differentiation of tendon-derived stem cells (TDSCs) could be enhanced through the sustained release of MCDs. The results showed that MCDs were successfully prepared and that the injectable hydrogel (M−S gel), which was composed of the successfully created MCDs and the SIS hydrogel, had good biocompatibility. The M−S gel markedly promoted the migration of TDSCs. IL-6 levels in the TDSCs were inhibited while those of IL-10 levels were promoted, demonstrating that the M−S gel had excellent anti-inflammatory and regenerative potential. Animal studies indicated that the M−S gel regulated the inflammatory microenvironment and promoted Achilles tendon repair. This study shows that MCDs can enhance the anti-inflammatory and tendon regeneration capacities of injectable SIS hydrogels, offering a new minimally invasive treatment for AT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of MCDs. (A, B) TEM. (C) Particle size distribution. (D) XRD, (E) FTIR and (F) XPS spectra of melatonin and MCDs. (G) C 1 s, (H) N 1 s and (I) O 1 s spectra of MCDs.

Characterizations of hydrogels. (A) Temperature-sensitive adhesive formation properties of SIS Gel and M−S Gel (B) SEM of SIS Gel and M−S Gel. (C) Swelling rate of SIS Gel and M−S Gel, (D) In vitro release of MCDs from SIS Gel and M−S Gel.

Biocompatibility of the hydrogels. (A) CCK-8 assay, (B) Live-dead cell staining (Green are living cells. Red are dead cells.) and (C) Cytoskeleton staining. (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Anti-inflammatory effects of different hydrogels on TDSCs. (A) The scratch area of different groups at 0, 12, 24, 36 and 48 h. (B) Quantitative analysis of migration rate. (C, D) Immunofluorescence staining of IL-6 and IL-10. (E, F) Gene expressions of IL-6 and IL-10. (G, H) Elisa tests of IL-6 and IL-10. (I, J) Quantitative analysis of the relative expression of IL-6 (I) and IL-10 (J) according to the immunofluorescence staining images. (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001).

In vivo repair of Achilles Tendinopathy by the M−S Gels in rats. (A) gross observation of the Achilles tendon. (B) Schematic diagram of the thickness and width of the tendon. (C, D) Quantitative analysis of width, thickness, and cross-sectional area of 1 and 4 weeks. (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001).

Evaluation of in vivo repair of Achilles Tendinopathy by the M−S Gels. (A) Ultrasound images of Achilles tendon area and blood flow. (B) Footprint photographs and (C) quantitative analysis of Achilles tendon functional index. (D-G) The mechanical properties of Achilles tendon treated by different hydrogels. (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001).

Histomorphology analysis. (A) H&E staining, (B) Masson staining, (C) Sirius red staining and (D) Safranin O-fast green staining. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Expression of inflammatory cytokines of Achilles tendon tissue. (A, B) Immunohistochemistry staining of IL-6 and IL-10, and (C) Immunofluorescence staining of p-p65 of the Achilles tendon, (D) Quantitative analysis of the relative expression of p-p65 according to the immunofluorescence staining. (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001).

Expression of tendon-related proteins of Achilles tendon tissue. Immunofluorescence staining of COL-Ⅰ (A) and COL-Ⅲ (B). Quantitative analysis of the relative expression of COL-Ⅰ (C) and COL-Ⅲ (D) according to the immunofluorescence staining images. Immunohistochemistry staining of SCX (E) and TNMD (F). (*, p < 0.05, **, p < 0.01, ***, p < 0.001 and ****, p < 0.0001).

Schematic representation of how M−S gel improves the inflammatory microenvironment and promotes tendon repair. The M−S gel releases MT-CDs to improve the inflammatory microenvironment of Achilles tendinopathy by inhibiting IL-6 expression and enhancing IL-10 expression. It also promotes Achilles tendon repair by increasing the expression of COL I, TNMD, and SCX and downregulating the expression of COL III. IL-6, interleukin-6; IL-10, interleukin-10; COL I, collagen I; COL Ⅲ, collagen Ⅲ; TNMD, tenomodulin; SCX, Scleraxis; and TDSCs, tendon-derived stem cell.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Achilles tendinopathy (AT), which is characterized by aseptic inflammation, is one of the most common types of tendon injury. Chronic inflammation can worsen AT progression and interfere with tendon repair, and most current anti-inflammatory treatments are associated with adverse effects. Therefore, improving the inflammatory microenvironment and promoting tenogenesis remain major challenges in AT treatment. In this study, melatonin carbon dots (MCDs) combined with an injectable porcine small intestinal submucosal hydrogel (SIS gel) were developed for the minimally invasive treatment of AT. The anti-inflammatory effects and tenogenic differentiation of tendon-derived stem cells (TDSCs) could be enhanced through the sustained release of MCDs. The results showed that MCDs were successfully prepared and that the injectable hydrogel (M−S gel), which was composed of the successfully created MCDs and the SIS hydrogel, had good biocompatibility. The M−S gel markedly promoted the migration of TDSCs. IL-6 levels in the TDSCs were inhibited while those of IL-10 levels were promoted, demonstrating that the M−S gel had excellent anti-inflammatory and regenerative potential. Animal studies indicated that the M−S gel regulated the inflammatory microenvironment and promoted Achilles tendon repair. This study shows that MCDs can enhance the anti-inflammatory and tendon regeneration capacities of injectable SIS hydrogels, offering a new minimally invasive treatment for AT.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Achilles tendinopathy (AT) is one of the most common clinical degenerative diseases of the tendonarising from prolonged high-impact exercise and tissue senescence, resulting in high morbidity and disability rates [1]. Degenerative tendinopathy occurs in 23.9 % of athletes and 5.9 % of the general population, with 2–3 per 1,000 adult patients experiencing Achilles tendon symptoms [2–4]. AT onset is usually characterized by localized pain, decreased mechanical properties, and limited movement, often accompanied by scar tissue formation [5]. AT may result in the patients being unable to work normally for a while, particularly those whose occupations require heavy use of the lower limbs, such as athletes, military personnel, and heavy laborers. In severe cases, this may lead to complete incapacitation, necessitating a prolonged period of rehabilitation and treatment. If AT is not treated in a timely manner, further damage to or even complete rupture of the Achilles tendon can occur, increasing the complexity and cost of treatment and potentially leading to long-term functional impairment and decreased quality of life [6,7]. Therefore, identifying timely and effective methods for treating AT is critical.

At present, the main treatment for AT is drug therapy [8,9], but there are still many problems, including the triggering of adverse effects, the inability to administer the correct dosage, and the inconsistency of treatment. Anti-inflammation is the focus of treatment, especially how to deal with aseptic inflammation of AT. Repeated irritation of the Achilles tendon following an injury creates an inflammatory microenvironment that triggers cellular metabolic disorders, tendon degeneration, and microstructural disorders [2]. In addition, subsequent to injury, immune cells are recruited and secrete inflammatory cytokines, inducing an inflammatory cascade that promotes the degeneration of tendon tissue and the progressive formation of calcified tissue [10]. Many inflammatory factors, such as interleukin-6 (IL-6) and interleukin-10 (IL-10), are reportedly involved in AT [6,11,12]. While nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), exemplified by celecoxib, can reduce the acute inflammatory response and provide analgesia, they have also been associated with a range of adverse effects, including tissue adhesions and delayed repair [13,14].

Regenerative medicine provides new ideas for the treatment of AT. With this method, the objective of AT treatment is not only to reduce the inflammation, but also accelerate tissue repair and prevent further deterioration. For example, Ruan et al. used platelet-rich plasma (PRP) to treat AT through anti-inflammatory measures [15]; however, this approach is inherently limited by autologous acquisition of PRP and viability maintenance over extended periods. Synthetic materials have also been used to repair tendon injuries, with PCL scaffolds representing one such example [16,17]. However, the degradation of certain synthetic materials can elicit inflammatory responses and even toxic side effects instead of facilitating tissue repair. Despite the promising results of stem cell therapy in treating tendon injuries [18], significant challenges remain in acquiring and preserving stem cells. Therefore, ways to inhibit inflammation and promote tendon regeneration are still needed.

Melatonin (N-acetyl-5-methoxytryptamine) is a natural metabolite both in plants and animals, which is known for its anti-inflammatory properties [19]. Many studies have demonstrated that melatonin reduces inflammation in smooth muscle cells, adipose tissue and bone tissue [20–22]. However, due to melatonin's high hydrophobicity of melatonin, its water solubility is low, and it tends to be poorly absorbed in the body, resulting in unsatisfactory therapeutic outcomes [23,24]. Carbon dots as a new type of nanomaterial have many advantages including excellent water solubility and biocompatibility [25–27]. In addition, carbon dots have been found to demonstrate multiple bioactivities. Li et al. reported that arginine carbon dots have immune regulatory and osteogenic characteristics [28]. In a recent study, Xin et al. demonstrated that melatonin-derived carbon dots (MCDs) possess favorable water solubility and biocompatibility, and exhibit potent anti-inflammatory properties in periodontal tissues [29]. Therefore, we speculate that MCDs may exert an anti-inflammatory effect in AT treatment.

It is also very important to develop a delivery system suitable for minimally invasive treatment. Because the Achilles tendon is an avascular tissue [30], injecting drugs directly into the disease site is a better way to improve therapeutic efficacy [31]. The use of appropriate slow-release materials effectively achieves local drug retention and improves retention time [32]. Given the tight tissue bonding and narrow surrounding cavity at the Achilles tendon, an injectable hydrogel that can achieve minimally invasive and shaping characteristics is a good candidate [33,34]. Hydrogel materials are derived from a range of sources. Hydrogels composed of natural polymers such as DNA, collagen, chitosan, alginate and hyaluronic acid have good biodegradability, satisfactory biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [35]. Among them, the small intestinal submucosal hydrogel (SIS gel) is an extracellular matrix hydrogel comprising 90 % collagen, which is similar in composition to the Achilles tendon [36,37]. Our previous study indicated that the extracellular matrix of the submucosa of the small intestine has good biocompatibility and facilitates Achilles tendon repair [32]. Therefore, the SIS gel is a good vehicle for MCDs and can be used to treat AT.

In this study, an injectable hydrogel (M−S gel) consisting of MCDs and SIS hydrogel was developed for the minimally invasive treatment of AT. The M−S gel delivered the nanomaterials to the tendon injury site via minimally invasive injection, and achieved significant anti-inflammatory effects through the slow release of MCDs. In addition, owing to its temperature-sensitive properties, the M−S gel can be formed into any shape in vivo and wrapped around the injury site, effectively expanding the application range and reducing Achilles tendon adhesions. The morphology and properties of MCDs and composite hydrogels were evaluated. The effects of the migration of the M−S gel on the tenogenic differentiation of tendon-derived stem cells (TDSCs) and the expression of inflammation-related genes and proteins were assessed. A rat AT model was ultimately constructed to evaluate the in vivo repair effect of the M−S gel (Scheme 1 ).

MCDs were obtained by further modification of the reported method, directly avoiding the use of HCL [29]. In summary, 250 mg of melatonin was solubilized in 10 mL of ultrapure water and heated at 180℃ for 6 h. An ultrasonic treatment of the MCD solution was performed after cooling to room temperature. The solution was subsequently subjected to dialysis in ultrapure water for a period of 48 h via a dialysis membrane (SP131060, Yuanye, China). MCDs were then obtained via lyophilization.

The morphology of MCDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; TECNAI G2 F20, FEI, USA). The diameter dispersion of the MCDs was quantified using a laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The molecular structures of melatonin and MCDs were detected using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) of melatonin and MCDs was also performed.

SIS was obtained through the mechanical treatment of fresh porcine small intestine and immersed in a 1:1 vol ratio of methanol and chloroform for 12 h. The SIS was then subjected to three washes with PBS and treated with 0.05 % trypsin solution containing 0.05 % EDTA for 12 h. Then, the SIS was immediately immersed in 0.5 % SDS solution for 4 h and 75 % alcohol for 20 min before being freeze-dried at − 80℃. The SIS was then minced and digested with 1 mg/mL pepsin. The mixture was fully digested by stirring with magnetic beads when the pH stabilized at 1–2. The pH was subsequently adjusted to neutral with 0.04 mol/L NaOH solution to obtain the SIS hydrogels. MCDs (150 μg, 300 μg, or 450 μg) were then added to the SIS gel (1 mL) to obtain different concentrations of the M−S gel. To observe the morphology of the hydrogels, the SIS and MCD-SIS hydrogels were immersed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde solution for 2 h. The samples were washed three times for 5 min and subsequently dehydrated in 50 %, 75 %, 80 %, 95 %, and 100 % ethanol sequentially for 20 min. The samples were dried and observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-70, Hitachi, Japan).
One milliliter of liquid gel precursor adjusted to neutrality was withdrawn into a glass bottle and placed in a 37 °C water bath for 5 min before the gel was converted to a solid state.

The hydrogels were submerged in deionized water, and the samples were removed at regular intervals to determine the hydrogel swelling ratio (SR). The mass of the hydrogel was then weighed, and the following formula equation was used to calculate the SR:
SR =
W 1 - W 0
/ W 0 × 100 % ,
where W1 is the mass after dissolution by immersion, g; W0 is the initial mass, g.

The SIS and M−S gels were prepared as cylinders and tested mechanically. The bottom diameter and height of the gels were first determined. The gels (n = 3) were subjected to compression at a rate of 0.01 mm/s (equivalent to 0.6 % strain/sec) until mechanical failure, and a universal material testing machine (TY-8000A, Tianyuan, China) was used. The initial 20 % strain in the stress–strain curve indicates the compression modulus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release of MCDs from the M−S gel
ce:section.ce:para: The release properties of MCDs released from 300 μg/mL M−S gel at different times were studied using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. In summary, the composite hydrogels were immersed in PBS at 37 °C. Quantitative analysis was conducted to determine the extent of liberation of microcarriers (MCDs) from composite hydrogels over various time points. The UV–visible absorption peaks of the MCDs at 320 nm at various concentrations (18.75, 37.5, 75, 150, and 300 μg/mL) were quantified to construct a standard curve. The MCDs released from the M−S gel were calculated according to the absorbance value using the standard curve.

The study used Sprague–Dawley rats aged between 6 and 8 weeks and weighing approximately 250 g. The isolation and culture of TDSCs from rats were conducted as previously described [38]. In brief, the Achilles tendons were obtained from the rats and cut into pieces. These tissues were digested with collagenase type I (C8140; Solarbio, Beijing, China) at a 3 mg/ml concentration for 4 h at 37 °C. Finally, the resulting cell suspensions were passed through a 70 µm cell strainer, centrifuged, and resuspended in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM; 2192823, Gibco, Shanghai, China) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS; #2128194, XP Biomed Ltd., Shanghai, China) and penicillin–streptomycin (#15140163, TRANSGEN, Beijing, China). The cells were cultured at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 3 consecutive days. All the experiments were conducted with P3 cells.

TDSCs (3 × 104) were inoculated in the lower chambers of 24-well Transwell plates, and the M−S gels with different concentrations of MCDs (150, 300, and 450 μg/mL MCDs) were positioned within the upper chambers of the plate. After 1, 3, and 5 days, cell viability was assessed using the CCK-8 test. Live–dead cell staining was also performed after culturing for 1, 3, and 5 days. For live–dead cell staining, the cells were treated with 2 M AMCalcein and 4 M EthD-1 in PBS at 37 °C, incubated for 30 min in the dark, and then visualized using fluorescence microscopy. The morphology of the cells on the different hydrogels was also observed. Different hydrogels (200 μL of each hydrogel) were placed in 24-well plates. TDSCs (5 × 104) were plated on the surface of the hydrogels and incubated for 24 h. The samples were immersed for 40 min in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution, followed by washing with PBS. The samples were then incubated with TRITC-phalloidin (CA1610, Solarbio, China) and DAPI (C0065, Solarbio, China) at room temperature. The cytoskeleton morphology was observed using fluorescence microscopy.

TDSCs (4 × 106) were inoculated in the lower chamber of a six-well Transwell plate, and when the cells were fully confluent, a blank cell-free area (width: 400 μm) was scratched at the bottom of each well. The cells were treated with 1 μg/mL LPS-containing medium (L8880, Solarbio, China) to simulate the cellular behavior of AT disease under inflammatory conditions. Then, the SIS and M−S gels (500 μL of each) were positioned within the upper chamber of the Transwell system. The cell culture media used in the SIS and M−S gel groups also contained 1 μg/mL LPS. The healing of the scratches was recorded at 12, 24, 36, and 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunofluorescence staining of IL-6 and IL-10
ce:section.ce:para: TDSCs (5 × 104) were inoculated in the lower chamber of a 24-well Transwell plate, and the SIS and M−S gels (200 μL of each) were introduced into the upper chamber of the Transwell plate and incubated in medium containing 1 μg/mL LPS. After coculture for 24 h, the cells were immersed in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 40 min. A rabbit anti-IL-6 monoclonal antibody (21865–1-AP, Proteintech, China) and a rabbit anti-IL-10 monoclonal antibody (21865–1-AP, Proteintech, China) were used as primary antibodies. A secondary antibody (SA00013-2, rabbit, Proteintech, China) (SA00013-4, rabbit, Proteintech, China) and DAPI (C0065, Solarbio, China) were added sequentially and visualized using fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ELISA detection of IL-6 and IL-10
ce:section.ce:para: TDSCs (1 × 105) were inoculated in the lower chamber of a 24-well Transwell plate, and the SIS and M−S gels (200 μL of each hydrogel) were introduced into the upper chamber. The cell medium contained 1 μg/mL LPS. After coculture for 24 h, the medium was aspirated for ELISA assessment of IL-6 and IL-10 using kits (RK00020, ABclonal, China and RK00050, ABclonal, China).

TDSCs (4 × 106) were inoculated in the lower chamber of a six-well Transwell plate. When the cells were fully grown, the SIS and M−S gels (200 μL of each) were introduced into the upper chamber of the Transwell system. The cell medium contained 1 μg/mL LPS. After cocultivation for 24 h, RNA was extracted as previously described [32]. Each group had three replicates. Total RNA was obtained from the cells using TRIzol reagent per the manufacturer's instructions (P40927, Takara, Dalian, China). cDNA synthesis was conducted on full-length cDNA samples using a kit (AT311, TRANSGEN, Beijing, China). qPCR was performed per the manufacturer’s protocols (P41014, Transgenics, Beijing, China). The gene expression levels of IL-6 and IL-10 were subsequently determined, and GAPDH was used as the housekeeping gene (Table 1 ).

The extraction of total cellular proteins was conducted using RIPA buffer (Solarbio, China), which included PMSF (Solarbio, China) and phosphatase inhibitors (New Cell & Molecular Biotechnology, China), and 20 μg of protein sample was subjected to electrophoresis. After membrane transfer, the membrane was blocked for 15 min with Rapid Closure Solution (New Cell & Molecular Biotechnology). The membranes were then incubated with the primary antibody at a temperature of 4 °C for 10 h. The membranes were then incubated with the secondary antibody for 2 h at 37 °C. The primary antibodies used included anti-β-actin antibody (Proteintech, China), anti-p-p65 (CST, USA), and anti-MC1R (Bioss, China).

The feeding, care, operation, and treatment of the experimental animals were conducted per the institution's guidelines. Approval for all experiments was granted by the Animal Research Ethics Committee of Ningbo University in China (Ethical Approval No. 13471). A total of 110 male Sprague–Dawley rats were purchased from Ningbo University Laboratory Animal Center, China. A total of 50 6-week-old rats were used for the generation of TDSCs. Sixty rats were used to assess the in vivo repair efficacy of the materials investigated in four groups: (1) the sham group without treatment; (2) the AT group, in which 30 μL of 10 mg/mL collagenase was injected for 1 week to construct an AT model; (3) the SIS group, in which 30 μL of 10 mg/mL collagenase was injected for 1 week, and then 200 μL of 10 mg/mL SIS hydrogel was injected; and (4) the M−S group, in which 30 μL of 10 mg/mL collagenase was injected for 1 week, and then 200 μL of 10 mg/mL SIS hydrogel containing 300 μg/mL MCDs was injected. The samples were taken 1 and 4 weeks after material implantation. A total of 30 μL of collagenase solution was injected into the middle of the Achilles tendon via a 1 mL syringe (Fig. S3). Similarly, 200 μL of SIS or M−S hydrogels were injected into the Achilles tendon as described above.

B-mode ultrasound was used to evaluate the extent of localized inflammation. Following the administration of general anesthesia, the Achilles tendon was examined using a 3.0 T scanner (Magnetar, Germany) with an appropriately sized and shaped coil. Sagittal and transverse T2-weighted imaging was performed in three rats per group (sequence parameters: repetition time, 1500 ms; echo time, 56 ms; field of view, 80 × 80 mm; substrate size, 320 × 256 mm; layer thickness, 2 mm; interlayer distance, 0.1 mm; and voxel size, 0.16 × 0.16 × 2.10 mm). Furthermore, evidence of blood flow at the site of injury to the Achilles tendon was observed. The surface area quantification method was used to quantify the area of the Achilles tendon. After the extent of neovascularization was determined using Doppler flow, quantitative analysis was performed using the Doppler flow surface area quantification (SAQ) method [39].

To evaluate the effects of the hydrogels on AT treatment, we assessed Achilles tendon mobility using the Achilles tendon functional index (AFI). The paws of the rats at 1 and 4 weeks of treatment were inked and the rats were allowed to walk in a straight line on white paper to enable footprint observation. The length of the footprints (anterior end of the third toe to the end of the footprints, PL), the spreading length of the toes (the first toe to the fifth toe, TS), and the spreading length of the intermediate toes (distance between the second and fourth toes, IT) were measured. Then, on the basis of the difference between the normal (N) and experimental (E) values, the three footprint size factors (PLF, TSF, and ITF) were derived according to the following formula and the AFI index for each group was obtained using the AFI calculation formula. (n = 5 per group).
PLF =
NPL - E P L
/ E P L
TSF =
ETS - N T S
/ N T S
ITF =
EIT - N I T
/ N I T
AFI = 74
PLF
+ 161
TSF
+ 48
ITF

Following collection, the samples were subjected to a series of staining procedures, including hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), Masson, Sirius red, Safranin O-fast green, immunohistochemical, and immunofluorescence. The samples were processed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, dehydrated, and embedded in paraffin. Longitudinal sections of the tendon were obtained in the coronal position, with a single slice thickness of 5 µm. The following staining techniques were used: H&E, Masson, Sirius red, Safranin O-fast green. Immunohistochemical and immunofluorescence staining were also performed. In brief, the samples were dewaxed, rehydrated, and stained. The tissue was initially treated with a 0.08 % trypsin solution at 37 °C (T8150, Solarbio, Beijing, China) for 2 h. Following the application of hydrogen peroxide (10011218, Sinopharm, Shanghai, China) and 10 % goat serum as a sealing agent, the tissues were immersed in solutions of anti-SCX (ab58655, Shanghai, China), anti-TNMD (ab203676, Shanghai, China), anti-rabbit COL I (A16891, AB clone, Wuhan, China), anti-rabbit COL III (A3795, AB clone, Wuhan, China), rabbit IL-6 monoclonal (21865-1-AP, Proteintech, China), rabbit IL-10 monoclonal (21865-1-AP, Proteintech, China), and rabbit anti-p-p65 (CST, USA, 1:1000) antibodies. Subsequently, secondary antibodies (CR2107161, Wuhan, China) and DAB (CR2103183, Wuhan, China) were added for immunohistochemical staining. A secondary antibody (SA00013-2, rabbit, Proteintech, China) (SA00013-4, rabbit, Proteintech, China) were added for immunofluorescence staining. DAPI (C0065, Solarbio, China) or hematoxylin was used for nuclear staining. All the images were captured using a microscope. The sham group was scored zero according to the histological grading scoring method [40]. Achilles tendon adhesions were scored independently by three people unaware of the adhesion, as previously described [41].

The biomechanical properties were evaluated using a biomechanical testing apparatus (#3366; INSTRON, Shanghai, China) (n = 5). The Achilles tendon was secured using the apparatus's clamps (Fig. S5). The range of the load cell of the test set was 0–200 N. The procedure's initial step was measuring the length and cross-sectional area of the Achilles tendon. The Achilles tendon cross-sectional area (S) was calculated using the following equation, and the AFI for each group was obtained using the area formula (n = 4 per group):
S = π × M 1 × M 2 / 4
where M1 is the width of the Achilles tendon, mm; M2 is the thickness of the Achilles tendon, mm.

The tendon was subjected to a constant tensile speed of 10 mm/min. The maximum load (N), stiffness (ST) and Young's modulus (YM) were calculated using the following equations:
ST = N / M 3
YM = σ / ε
ε = D / O L × 100 %
where M3 is the displacement at the maximum load, mm; σ is the stresses, MPa; D is the Deformation, mm; OL is the Original Length, mm.

The statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 8.0. Data from rats that died during the experiment were excluded. A series of tests and analyses were conducted on the dataset, including the Kruskal–Wallis test, to examine data that did not adhere to the usual assumption of normality. In instances where the data exhibited consistency with a normal distribution and where a homogeneity of variance test was deemed appropriate, Student's t test was used for datasets comprising two groups, whereas one-way ANOVA was used for datasets comprising three or more groups. Statistical significance was determined at p < 0.05 level, and the level of significance is expressed as *, **, ***, and **** for p < 0.05, p < 0.01, p < 0.001, and p < 0.0001, respectively. The results are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Each group was subjected to at least three analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the MCDs and composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: MCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal synthesis method. The TEM results revealed that the MCDs were spherical (Fig. 1 A), and the lattice width was 0.006 nm (Fig. 1B). Particle size analysis demonstrated that the MCDs exhibited a uniform and stable diameter, with an average value of 2.1 nm (Fig. 1C). The XRD results revealed that melatonin had obvious peaks at 2θ values of 10.8°, 16.4°, and 26.1°, indicating that it has a crystal structure (Fig. 1D). The MCDs also presented similar peaks at these positions, demonstrating that some of the structure of melatonin was retained. The FTIR results (Fig. 1E) revealed that melatonin and MCD presented absorption peaks at approximately 3287, 1594, 1491, 1433, and 1211 cm−1, which were attributed to the presence of N-H, C=C, C-N, and C-O-C, respectively. In addition, the MCD produced several new functional groups, such as O-H at approximately 2933 cm−1, C=O/C=N at 1620–1720 cm−1, C-O at 1306 cm−1, and O-H at 926 cm−1. Moreover, the nanostructure and elemental composition of the MCD were analyzed using XPS. The spectra indicated that the MCDs were primarily composed of C, N, and O with atomic ratios of 72.26 %, 12.22 %, and 15.53 %, respectively (Fig. 1F). The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1G) showed three different types of C, namely, C-H (284.4 eV), C-N (285.2 eV), and C=O (286.1 eV). In addition, the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1H) exhibited two peaks at 400.1 eV and 400.8 eV, corresponding to C–N and N–C=O, respectively. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1I) of the MCDs showed two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.1 eV, corresponding to C=N and C-O-C, respectively.

The SIS hydrogel (Fig. S1) was successfully prepared in accordance with the preceding steps. The MCD was incorporated into the SIS hydrogel, forming the M−S gel (MCD-SIS hydrogel). This gel exhibited temperature-sensitive gelation at 37 °C (Fig. 2 A). The lyophilized SIS and M−S gels were observed using a scanning electron microscope (Fig. 2B). A comparative analysis of the pore structures of the SIS gels indicated that the incorporation of MCDs did not result in any notable alterations. A comparative analysis of the swelling properties of the SIS gels was conducted using a swelling test, which demonstrated that the incorporation of MCDs did not markedly influence the swelling behavior of the gels (Fig. 2C). The compressive moduli of the SIS and M−S gels (Fig. S2) were compared using a universal material testing machine, and no significant difference was found between the two. The cumulative release test (Fig. 2D) demonstrated that the M−S gel released 79 % of the melatonin-CDs within 14 days, indicating that the composite hydrogel could release MCD in a controlled and gradual manner. These findings substantiate the successful preparation of the MCD-SIS composite hydrogel with a sustained, slow-release function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell morphology and viability of the composite hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: We evaluated the biological compatibility of the composite hydrogel. Rat TDSCs were seeded on the surface of the composite hydrogel and subsequently cultured for 1, 3, or 5 days. The findings of the CCK-8 assay demonstrated that the SIS gel, 150 μg/mL M−S gel, and 300 μg/mL M−S gel promoted cell growth, and the 300 μg/mL M−S gel was the most effective. However, cell proliferation was inhibited on the 450 μg/mL M−S gel (Fig. 3 A). To simulate in vivo hydrogel release, we cultured TDSCs at the bottom of a Transwell plate, and different hydrogels were placed in the top chamber. A live–dead staining procedure was conducted after 1, 3, and 5 days of culturing. The results showed that the 150 μg/mL and 300 μg/mL M−S gels did not affect cell proliferation or viability. However, the 150 μg/mL M−S gel demonstrated obvious cytotoxicity (Fig. 3B). We seeded TDSCs onto plates (normal group) containing hydrogels. Cytoskeletal staining demonstrated that TDSCs exhibited robust adhesion and spreading on the SIS gel and 150 μg/mL or 300 μg/mL M−S gel, indicating favorable biocompatibility. The 450 μg/mL M−S gel inhibited the growth of TDSCs and was less biocompatible (Fig. 3C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory effects of M−S gels on TDSCs in an inflammatory microenvironment
ce:section.ce:para: The TDSCs were cultured in the lower compartment of a Transwell plate, with the hydrogels placed in the upper compartment. LPS (1 μg/mL) was introduced into the culture medium, after which the cells were subsequently cultured for an additional 24 h to induce an inflammatory microenvironment. A scratch assay was performed to assess cell migration. To perform this experiment, scratches were created and incubation was continued. In the LPS-induced inflammatory environment, the cell healing rate was significantly slowed down in the LPS group. In contrast, cell migration was faster in both the SIS and M−S gel groups than in the LPS group when co-cultured. In addition, it was observed that the cell migration rate of the M−S gel group was faster than that of the SIS gel group. The migration rates of cells in the Normal group and the M−S gel group were similar after 48 h (Fig. 4 B), and the M−S gel could significantly improve the migration ability of TDSCs in the inflammatory environment. The immunofluorescence staining results revealed that IL-6, a proinflammatory factor, was expressed at the maximum level in the LPS group and at the minimum level in the M−S Gel group (Fig. 4C). The expression of IL-6 in the M−S Gel group was similar to that in the Normal group. qPCR results revealed that the M−S hydrogel significantly inhibited the expression of IL-6 (Fig. 4E). An ELISA of the cell supernatant revealed that the concentration of IL-6 in the M−S Gel group was markedly lower than that observed in the LPS group (Fig. 4G). IL-10, an anti-inflammatory factor, was expressed at the highest level in the M−S Gel group and at the lowest level in the LPS group, which was the opposite of the IL-6 results (Fig. 4D). qPCR results revealed that the M−S gel significantly promoted the expression of IL-10 (Fig. 4F). ELISAs demonstrated that the concentration of IL-10 (Fig. 4H) in the M−S Gel group was markedly elevated compared with that in the LPS group.
Moreover, the western blotting results revealed that the expression of p-p65 was significantly suppressed in the M−S group and that the expression of MC1R was significantly increased (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo repair of Achilles tendinopathy via the M−S gels
ce:section.ce:para: Animal specimens were collected 1 and 4 weeks after surgery. Gross photographs of each group of Achilles tendons are shown in Fig. 5 A. The sham Achilles tendon was clearly defined, and the AT group presented severe injury at 1 week, with the Achilles tendon surface wrapped in opaque yellowish tissue and the tendon bundle adhering to the surrounding tissues and not clearly visible. However, the SIS and M−S groups showed a slight therapeutic effect, with a significant decrease in the formation of abnormal tissues, and the tendon bundle could be clearly observed. In both the SIS and M−S groups, the anomalous tissue observed at the surface of the Achilles tendon had resolved at 4 weeks. In the AT group, however, the Achilles tendon bundles were fused and could not be separated. The M−S gel had a significant effect, with the Achilles tendon recovering to a degree close to that of the Sham group. The thickness and width of the Achilles tendon were measured, and its cross-sectional area was then analyzed (Fig. 5B). The findings indicated that the thickness, width, and cross-sectional area of the Achilles tendon in the AT group were significantly greater than those in the Sham group. Moreover, the recovery of the Achilles tendon in both the SIS and M−S groups was more pronounced than that observed in the AT group (Fig. 5C–D). The therapeutic effect of the M−S hydrogel was more pronounced than that of the other hydrogels and similar to that of the Sham group.

We further examined ultrasound imaging, motor function, and mechanical properties after repair with the M−S gel. The ultrasound imaging results indicated that the Achilles tendon area of the AT, SIS and M−S groups increased at 1 week, with the most obvious increase in the AT group (Fig. 6 A). The blood flow in the lesion also significantly increased, potentially reflecting changes in response to inflammation. At 4 weeks, the Achilles tendon areas of the SIS and M−S groups were smaller than those of the AT group and close to those of the sham group. Blood flow was reduced in the SIS and M−S groups; in particular, the M−S hydrogel group returned to the same level as the sham group. The results of the quantitative ultrasound imaging analyses also support these findings (Fig. S4). The results of the gait test are presented in Fig. 6B. The footprint lengthened significantly in the AT group at 1 week, which may be due to the functional impairment caused by the disease. The shortened footprint length in the M−S hydrogel group was evident at 4 weeks. The AFI serves as a metric for assessing motor functional recovery following Achilles tendon injury. We calculated the AFI of each group by measuring the overall length of the footprint and the toe extension length using the formula and found that the AFI of the AT group was significantly reduced at 1 and 4 weeks (Fig. 6C). The AFI of the SIS and M−S groups increased slightly at 1 week, and the therapeutic efficacy of the M−S gel was better than that of the SIS gel at 4 weeks and was more closely related to that of the sham group. The results of mechanical tests using a universal tester revealed that AT reduces the maximum load, Young’s modulus (Fig. 6D–G), and stiffness (Fig. S6) of the Achilles tendon. Furthermore, the mechanical characteristics of the Achilles tendon were notably enhanced following the administration of the M−S gel.

To elucidate the biological mechanisms involved in the healing process, we evaluated the effects of the SIS and M−S gels on AT using H&E, Masson, and Sirius red staining. The H&E staining results demonstrated that the collagen fibers of the Achilles tendon in the AT group exhibited a distinct degree of disorganization at the 1-week mark (Fig. 7 A). The SIS and M−S gels promoted the recovery of injured tendons, which exhibited regularity of collagen fibers at 1 week. However, notable differences were observed among the SIS, M−S, and sham groups. Four weeks later, the collagen fibers in the SIS and M−S groups became more regular. Among them, the M−S group exhibited the best recovery, approaching the performance level of the sham group. Furthermore, the SIS gel demonstrated a degree of efficacy in promoting tendon recovery, albeit to a lesser extent. The Masson staining results demonstrated that the AT group exhibited a notable decrease in collagen fiber integrity and substantial impairment of ECM tissue at 1 week (Fig. 7B). In contrast, the SIS and M−S groups exhibited slight recovery. At 4 weeks, there was minimal improvement in collagen fiber damage in the AT group, whereas the SIS group exhibited partial improvement. However, the difference between these groups and the Sham group remained evident. The efficacy of the M−S gel was notable, as it was markedly similar to that of the Sham group. The Sirius red staining results were comparable to the Masson staining results, with a discernible reduction in the number of collagen fibers evident across all the experimental groups at 1 week (Fig. 7C). The damage to the collagen fibers was partially improved in the AT and SIS groups at 4 weeks. The recovery of tendons in the M−S group was more obvious than that in the AT and SIS groups and closer to that in the sham group. Safranin O-fast green staining showed that the collagen fibres in the AT group were disarranged and reduced. After treatment, the M−S group basically returned to normal (Fig. 7D). H&E staining of the animals' major organs demonstrated that M−S Gel has excellent biosafety (Fig. S7).

IL-6 and IL-10 are genes related to inflammation. It was found that IL-6 expression was observed in the AT, SIS and M−S groups, while its expression was more obvious in the AT group than in the SIS and M−S groups. The level of IL-6 in the SIS and M−S groups was similar. After 4 weeks, the level of IL-6 was reduced in the AT, SIS, and M−S groups. Among all groups, IL-6 expression was the lowest in the M−S group (Fig. 8 A). At 1 week, the M−S group presented the highest level of IL-10 presence (Fig. 8B). After 4 weeks, there was no expression of IL-10 levels in all groups except in the M−S gel group, where IL-10 expression still persisted (Fig. 8B). These findings indicate that the M−S gel effectively reduced IL-6 expression and increased IL-10 expression, suppressing inflammation and promoting tissue repair. P65 is a gene related to the NF-κB signaling pathway. The phosphorylation of p65 is a prerequisite for the activation of the NF-κB signaling pathway. The immunofluorescence staining results demonstrated that the p-p65 levels at 1 week were increased in the AT and SIS groups but decreased in the M−S group. At 4 weeks, p-p65 expression was reduced in most groups. p-p65 expression in the M−S Gel group was nearly undetectable, while the maximum expression was still observed in the AT group.

At 4 weeks, there was a decrease in the expression of type III collagen across all groups, with the AT group demonstrating the most pronounced expression (Fig. 9 B). The M−S group exhibited minimal expression of type Ⅲ collagen. Moreover, type Ⅰ collagen was expressed in all the groups at 1 week, with the lowest expression observed in the AT group (Fig. 9A). Type Ⅰ collagen expression increased significantly in the M−S group at 4 weeks. The SCX and TNMD genes are associated with the differentiation of tendons. An increase in SCX expression was observed in the M−S group compared with the AT group at 1 week. However, at 4 weeks, SCX expression decreased in all groups, which may indicate that the SCX gene is associated with early Achilles tendon injury repair (Fig. 9E). Conversely, TNMD expression was low in all groups at 1 week, yet the M−S group presented higher TNMD expression than did the AT group. After 4 weeks, there was essentially no change in TNMD expression in the AT group, whereas TNMD continued to increase and exhibited the highest expression in the M−S group (Fig. 9F).

A new minimally invasive treatment for AT is a major clinical need. Anti-inflammatory effects and the promotion of tendon regeneration are two important goals of AT treatment [42]. Compared with traditional drug therapy, minimally invasive administration not only improves drug efficacy and avoids the side effects of systemic administration, but also avoids causing secondary tissue damage and significant patient pain. However, there is no suitable minimally invasive treatment system for AT. The rise of tissue engineering and regenerative medicine has resulted in new possibilities for treating AT [43,44].

We propose the use of MCDs to modulate inflammation and promote regeneration. MCDs were successfully synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method, and the use of HCL in the synthesis process was avoided for improved safety compared with the previously reported methods. We used SIS gels as carriers for delivering MCDs because they have temperature-sensitive gel-forming properties and excellent biocompatibility [45]. Combining MCDs with SIS gels to form M−S gels retains the original properties of the SIS gels and effectively slow down the release of the MCDs. Compared with the melatonin [46], the MCDs generate some new functional groups and anti-inflammatory effects, and the M−S gel can be injected directly into the injury site, achieving the goal of minimally invasive treatment.

Long-term chronic inflammation has been reported to interfere with and inhibit the activity of TDSCs [47]. We constructed an inflammatory microenvironment by treating cells with LPS. The in vitro results revealed that TDSCs presented decreased IL-6 expression, elevated IL-10 expression, and decreased p-p65 expression after M−S coculture. The p65 gene is associated with the NF-κB signaling pathway [48], and the phosphorylation of p65 is a prerequisite for activating the NF-κB signaling pathway [49]. These findings suggest that the M−S gel may enhance the anti-inflammatory ability of TDSCs and inhibit the NF-κB signaling pathway, which is beneficial for tendon repair. After co-culturing M−S hydrogel with TDSCs, it was found that MC1R of TDSCs was activated, which suggested that M−S hydrogel might regulate the anti-inflammatory ability of TDSCs by activating MC1R. Our study demonstrated that hydrogels containing MCD could regulate the expression of inflammatory factors and promote their regeneration

Although many anti-inflammatory drugs have been proposed to improve AT, most of these drugs trigger adverse reactions and tissue adhesion [50]. Therefore, the anti-inflammatory and regenerative effects of the M−S hydrogel were further evaluated in a rat AT model. The animal test results revealed that the Achilles tendons of the rats in the M−S group recovered considerably without significant adhesions. Morphological examination of the Achilles tendon, ultrasonic evaluation, footprints, and mechanical analyses revealed significant Achilles tendon repair in the M−S group. Histological examination, immunohistochemical staining, and immunofluorescence staining revealed that the expression of IL-6 was lower, that of IL-10 was higher, and that of p-p65 was more obviously decreased in the M−S group compared with the other groups during the same period. Moreover, the M−S group expressed more type I collagen and less type III collagen, and the expression of SCX and TNMD was greater. These findings suggest that the M−S gel may promote the tenogenic differentiation of TDSCs and accelerate the repair of Achilles tendon injury by regulating the inflammatory microenvironment and extracellular matrix remodeling. Additionally, some studies have demonstrated collagen fibre damage in patients with AT [51,52]. Safranin O-fast green staining revealed that the M−S hydrogel composite system promoted collagen fibre repair [53].

In conclusion, the M−S gel is an innovative system that combines nanomaterials and hydrogels. The anti-inflammatory capacity of MCDs and their role in promoting tendon regeneration were identified. This study has provided a protocol for developing M−S gels with easy-to-obtain raw materials, a simple preparation process, and easy preservation; furthermore, the gels induce fewer side effects than existing anti-inflammatory drugs, PRP, and cell delivery therapies. Moreover, this study has provided valuable insights into the safe and effective minimally invasive treatment of AT. However, further studies are clearly needed to elucidate the anti-inflammatory properties of MCDs. In addition, the efficacy of M−S hydrogels has only been tested in rats. Further validation of its reliability in larger animal models is needed.

In this study, we have proposed an innovative approach for the minimally invasive treatment of AT with an injectable SIS gel containing MCDs. MCDs were introduced into the SIS hydrogels to modulate inflammation and tenogenic differentiation of TDSCs, promoting tendon repair. The results demonstrated that the expression of IL-6 and IL-10 in TDSCs was inhibited and promoted, respectively, indicating that the M−S gel exhibited excellent anti-inflammatory and regenerative potential. In the rat AT model, damaged tendon structure and motor function were restored by the implantation of the M−S gel through the inhibition of inflammation and promotion of stem cell tendon differentiation in the body. This study’s findings indicate that the M−S gel with anti-inflammatory and tendon regeneration abilities may be applied in the minimally invasive treatment of AT.
